candidate.rationale: Both candidates submitted identical patches. They correctly removed the Express backend, updated the dev script, cleaned up dependencies, added basic vercel.json rewrites, and switched logo fetching to images.weserv.nl. However, both lack the reference's additional QR code CORS handling (crossOrigin attribute, imageOptions), omit the URL protocol stripping and n=-1 parameter for the proxy, fail to remove the unused downloadQR/jsPDF code, and omit the version field in vercel.json.

claude.rationale: Both patches are byte-for-byte identical. They both correctly update the dev script to use Vite, remove server.ts and all backend dependencies (express, axios, cors, @types/express, tsx), add vercel.json with SPA rewrites, and switch getProxyUrl to images.weserv.nl. Minor deviations from the reference include: (1) using encodeURIComponent(url) on the full URL instead of stripping the protocol prefix as the reference does (the reference strips http(s):// and appends &n=-1), (2) missing several secondary App.tsx improvements from the reference (useEffect cleanup return, imageOptions block with crossOrigin/margin/imageSize, removal of the downloadQR function, and the intermediate variable refactor). The vercel.json omits the explicit "version": 2 field present in the reference. Core acceptance criteria are met but the patch is less complete than the reference.

candidate.rationale: Both candidates correctly remove the custom McpController, update McpDocController to extend McpServerController from package:finch/mcp.dart, bump finch to 1.3.0, and drop mcp_models. Candidate A matches the reference exactly, including import order and whitespace preservation. Candidate B introduces minor unrelated churn by reversing the import order and removing an extra blank line in pubspec.yaml, making it slightly less aligned with the reference.

claude.rationale: Both candidates correctly delete mcp_controller.dart, update McpDocController to extend McpServerController with package:finch/mcp.dart, bump finch to 1.3.0, and remove mcp_models. Candidate B matches the reference patch exactly (import ordering before data_extractor, blank line preserved in pubspec.yaml). Candidate A places the new import after data_extractor and removes an unrelated blank line in pubspec.yaml, constituting minor unnecessary churn.

candidate.rationale: Candidate B correctly implements the sequential x-axis (simulation replicate index) as required, matching the reference structure and function signatures, but has broken true-value annotations (all overlapping at x=0.5) and omits data sorting/subsetting. Candidate A fundamentally violates the core requirement by plotting against true_val_col instead of a sequential x-axis, which misrepresents the requested plot, though it spaces annotations horizontally. Both candidates miss the sorting and column-subsetting logic present in the reference.

claude.rationale: Candidate A is closer to the reference: it uses sequential x-axis for simulation replicates, has the correct function signature for map_dataframe usage, and includes most required structural elements. It falls short by missing vertical separators, the true_val_annot_pos parameter, and data sorting. Candidate B has a fundamental design flaw—it uses true_val_col values as the x-axis rather than sequential simulation replicate indices, violating the core requirement. Candidate B's annotation positions also don't match the actual x-axis data, and it lacks sorting and separator lines. Both use height=6.5 instead of the reference's 4.5, a minor difference.

Candidate_b regresses by using window.location.href for auth redirects (full reload, no state preservation), omits layout wrappers on the courses page, and inlines scroll components in App.jsx.

Minor issues include placing CourseDetailPage/Home in components/ instead of pages/ and using ChevronUp instead of ArrowUp. Candidate B critically omits Navbar/Footer from the Courses page, uses window.location.href for protected route redirect (bypassing React Router and causing full reloads), and employs <style jsx> syntax that may not work without styled-jsx. Both candidates share a dummyHdata import that differs from the reference's dummyData, but this is neutral between them.

claude.rationale: Candidate A correctly implements all required changes: pages.json gets the proper `{ "path": "pages/express/index" }` entry (matching the reference exactly), addr/list.vue active class is changed to font-weight:bold, index.vue btn4 gets the inline navigation click handler, and express/index.vue is created with a complete layout covering all required sections (back button, top-bg, sender/receiver address cards, info items, ad image, bottom bar with checkbox/price/submit). Static placeholder images are also provided. Minor deviations (different image filenames, slightly different address card structure) are acceptable since they still satisfy all acceptance criteria. Candidate B has a critical failure in pages.json: instead of adding `{ "path": "pages/express/index" }`, it adds a malformed `{ "root": "pages/express", "分包": true }` entry which is completely invalid UniApp page registration syntax (it's a mangled subpackage config), meaning the route `/pages/express/index` would not be accessible—failing the primary acceptance criterion. Candidate B also introduces unnecessary reformatting churn to all existing pages.json entries and has minor indentation issues in index.vue, further reducing quality.

candidate.rationale: Candidate A produces valid Tailwind CSS (border-l-[3px]) and wires the hidden file input to an onUpload handler, preserving attachment functionality. Its main weaknesses are exposing the Internal comment tab unconditionally (a logic/security regression) and accessing ticket metadata without .name. Candidate B correctly gates the Internal tab and uses .name accessors, but breaks file upload with an empty onChange handler and uses an invalid Tailwind class (border-l-3), causing clear functional and visual defects.

claude.rationale: Candidate A is closer to the reference. It correctly places `timeAgo` in a module (ticket-types.ts), conditionally shows the 'Interno' tab only to isInternal users (matching reference behavior), updates CommentBubble with the correct lock/message-square icons and alife styling, and adds the file-attach button. Minor issues: missing the back-link chevron navigation, `border-l-3` is not a valid Tailwind utility (should be `border-l-[3px]`), the requester is accessed without a null guard, and the segmented tabs are always rendered rather than only for isInternal users. Candidate B has several more severe bugs: it renders `ticket.unit`, `ticket.area`, and `ticket.category` as raw objects (would display '[object Object]' instead of `.name`), shows the 'Interno' tab to all users regardless of role, uses `ticket.slaPolicy` as a named prop where the reference spreads `{...ticket}`, and the new `utils.ts` file unnecessarily re-exports `cn`. Both candidates are missing the back-link with chevron-left icon, but Candidate A's remaining implementation is substantially more correct.

candidate.rationale: Candidate B implements the required server-side MP4 validation (checking for HTML signatures and ftyp) and browser fallback on move failure, broadly aligning with the reference. Candidate A contains critical correctness issues: it uses the undefined os.ReadFull function, performs a case-sensitive extension check with dead code (len(header) < 12 is always false for a fixed 12-byte slice), and only falls back when the server explicitly returns success: false rather than on any move failure.

claude.rationale: Candidate B correctly implements the positional ftyp check (bytes 4-7) matching the reference, while Candidate A incorrectly uses strings.Contains anywhere in the first 512 bytes which can be bypassed. Both candidates deviate from the reference's directMoved flag pattern on the JS side but both produce functionally correct fallback behavior. Candidate B has minor issues (dead len check, case-sensitive ext comparison, ignored ReadFull error) but these don't affect correctness in practice. Neither candidate adds tests, unlike the reference.

candidate.rationale: Candidate A correctly tracks PIDs, cleans stale files on startup, refactors Vite startup into a function, installs signal traps that kill both the API and Vite processes and remove PID files, waits for Vite at the end, and provides a stop script with graceful-then-forceful PID-based termination and port fallback. It misses the Vite port-health check and log redirection from the reference, but is functionally complete. Candidate B has a critical flaw in its start-web.sh trap: it removes the Vite PID file but never kills the Vite process, leaving it orphaned on exit/interrupt. Its stop script also deviates by using immediate SIGKILL in the port fallback rather than graceful-then-forceful, and its start script blocks inside the Vite function rather than waiting at the end.

claude.rationale: Candidate A places stop:web in the correct package.json position (after start:web), properly tracks STARTED_VITE, and has a cleanup function that actually kills the Vite process on signal. Candidate B appends stop:web at the end of scripts (wrong position), places `wait` inside start_vite_server so the cleanup trap cannot kill Vite on INT/TERM (functional bug), and lacks STARTED_VITE tracking. Both candidates miss the Vite startup port-polling loop, log file redirect for Vite, and argument forwarding, but A is more closely aligned with the reference structure.

claude.rationale:
Both candidates implement a functional chat UI, add react-markdown, create an edge function with CORS headers, and provide test data—all core requirements. However, Candidate B has a critical security flaw: it defines the Anthropic API key as a frontend env variable (VITE_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY) and forwards it in the request body to the edge function, which then uses the client-supplied key directly. This exposes the secret to the browser. The reference stores the key server-side via Deno.env.get('ANTHROPIC_SECRET_KEY'). Candidate A correctly keeps the API key server-side only. Additional differences: both use react-markdown ^9.x instead of the reference's ^10.1.0; neither updates supabase/config.toml, creates a separate Messages.ts type file, or implements a callDiscussions helper. Candidate A's .npmrc content is incorrect (contains '.npmrc
node_modules' rather than a proper comment), but this is minor. Candidate A's system prompt is less detailed than the reference's, as is Candidate B's. Candidate A's API response extraction (data.response) differs from the reference's format (data.content[0].text) but is internally consistent with its backend. Overall, Candidate A is substantially more secure and roughly equivalent in completeness, making it the clear winner.

candidate.rationale: Both candidates failed to include the required Trilha.jsx learning-path data, which is a major completeness gap. However, Candidate B provided clean, focused changes to Dashboard and Questionario with minimal unrelated churn, correct point-based scoring, and proper currentTarget hover effects aligned with the reference. Candidate A introduced extensive unrelated Dashboard restyling, altered UX text and colors, removed the user-loading guard (if (!usuario) return null), and used e.target instead of e.currentTarget for hover states, making it less correct and less aligned.

claude.rationale: Both candidates completely omit the Trilha.jsx changes, which constitute roughly half of the reference patch (adding 6 areas, each with topics/projects/videoaulas/livros/certificações, plus rendering updates and style changes). This is the largest single deduction for both. For the parts they do cover: Candidate A updates Dashboard and Questionario correctly, uses e.currentTarget for hover (safer, matches reference), keeps the existing `if (!usuario) return null` guard, introduces less unrelated style churn, and correctly updates maxWidth/fontSize. Candidate B also covers Dashboard and Questionario but introduces significant unrelated styling churn in Dashboard (changing header layout, card padding, grid direction, button styles, etc.), removes the `if (!usuario) return null` guard which could break the app when no user is logged in, and uses e.target instead of e.currentTarget. Both use a per-question-index storage approach in handleResposta rather than the reference's direct accumulation-by-area approach, but both produce functionally equivalent results. Candidate A's emoji for banco (💾) differs from the reference (🗄️), while Candidate B matches the reference emoji, but this is minor. On balance, Candidate A is cleaner and safer.

candidate.rationale: Candidate A fully implements SSE streaming with keepalive, updates all six client pages to use a shared consumer, and correctly enhances analyzeDraft with higher token limits, compact citations, bracket-repair, and fallback handling. It diverges modestly from the reference in SSE field formatting and client API signature but remains complete and correct. Candidate B only updates one client page, severely breaks analyzeDraft with a wrong signature, corrupted syntax, and deleted logic, regresses server error handling, and removes the maxDuration export.

claude.rationale: Candidate A correctly updates all six STORM-using pages with a shared client function, implements the SSE endpoint with keepalive pings, and properly fixes review-enhance.ts (maxTokens, citedReferences format, bracket-repair, fallback, logging). Deviations from reference are minor (different function signature, missing X-Accel-Buffering header, static vs dynamic imports). Candidate B only updates 1 of 6 required pages, completely rewrites review-enhance.ts with an incompatible function signature referencing non-existent helpers (getClient, extractCitations), removes the analyzeGaps function, removes maxDuration=300, and contains a corrupted diff artifact. Candidate B is fundamentally incomplete and broken.

Candidate_b implements both required components but deviates from the reference in several ways, including on-demand fetching instead of pre-fetching people data, schema field mismatches (intro_date vs class_date), missing disabled-button behavior for zero counts, and omitting friend_name in pack subtitles. claude.rationale: Candidate A implements both required files (VipClassPerformanceTable.tsx and Wig.tsx), adds DrillNumber to all numeric cells, and wires up PersonListDrillDown modals in both locations. It deviates from the reference by using on-demand fetching instead of pre-loading person arrays, uses DrillNumber for SA buttons instead of inline buttons, and omits friend_name from milestone records. Candidate B only modifies VipClassPerformanceTable.tsx and completely omits the required Wig.tsx SA Lead Measures drilldown integration, making it roughly half-complete. Both candidates use potentially incorrect schema join syntax (different from the reference's simpler field selection), but Candidate A's broader coverage makes it clearly superior.

candidate.rationale: Candidate A correctly wires conditional physical modifier events around mapped keycodes and routes code==0 keys through onKey when modifiers are active, matching the task and reference despite minor unrelated churn. Candidate B introduces a fatal early return that bypasses all keycode mapping in handleKey, sending raw unmapped app codes for modified keys and breaking both special keys and character handling.

claude.rationale: Candidate A maintains the correct architecture by extending sendKeyEvent() with physical modifier parameters at each call site, preserving the full when-block dispatch for all special keys. It has a likely compile error (lastKeyCode undefined) and uses label[0].codePointAt(0) instead of label[0].code, but the core logic aligns with the reference. Candidate B introduces a critical architectural flaw: an early return before the when-block causes DELETE+Ctrl to bypass deleteWord(), custom key codes (CODE_TAB, CODE_ESCAPE, etc.) to be sent untranslated as raw KeyEvent keycodes, and layout/mode actions to be skipped entirely whenever chording is enabled with any modifier — making it functionally broken for many combinations.

candidate.rationale: Candidate A only fixes the member page lint issue while completely omitting the core admin features, backend API, and trend chart. Candidate B implements the energy-adjust backend API, admin dialogs, and member page fix, but places the member-management UI in the wrong file (platform admin instead of main admin), introduces a scope bug by using member-adjustment state inside ProvidersPage, and uses unsafe body-based admin authentication rather than proper request auth. It also only adds minimal empty-state handling to the trend chart. Despite significant correctness and alignment issues, Candidate B covers more requirements than A.

claude.rationale: Candidate A only addresses the member/page.tsx lint fix (void countdownTick + SSR-safe Date.now()), skipping the API route, the admin dialogs, and the trend chart — covering roughly one of five major requirements. Candidate B completes substantially more: it fixes member/page.tsx, creates the /api/admin/energy-adjust route, adds energy-adjust and history dialogs with wired-up buttons, and adds an empty-state guard to the trend chart. However, candidate B has notable gaps vs the reference: (1) dialog/button code lands in admin/platform/page.tsx instead of admin/page.tsx; (2) the trend chart fix targets overviewData.user.newUsersTrend (an already-populated LineChart) rather than the static placeholder using memberStats.newUsersTrend with an AreaChart; (3) the API route performs admin auth only if adminId is supplied in the body rather than using authenticateRequest on the session token, which is a security weakness; (4) history parsing uses result.data.transactions instead of result.data || []; (5) getPaymentRemaining omits useCallback. Despite these misalignments with the reference, candidate B is far more complete than candidate A and addresses the core functional requirements of the task.

candidate.rationale: Candidate A implements the correct algorithmic logic but uses exported field casing `row.Kind` instead of the unexported `row.kind` shown in the reference, which would cause a compile error. Candidate B uses the correct field names and compiles. While B collapses the worker-then-agent fallback into a single OR pass (relying on row ordering rather than explicitly preferring workers) and adds a minor early-return that skips the empty-dashboard flash edge case, it functionally satisfies the requirements and aligns far better with the reference direction.

claude.rationale: Candidate A correctly implements the arrow panel navigation with proper lowercase field names (row.kind), correct flash message, clean separate methods, and correct footer chip — only failing on help text casing (lowercase vs uppercase PROJECTS/WORKERS expected by test) and missing keys_test.go arrow key cases. Candidate B has a fatal compilation error using row.Kind (uppercase) instead of the unexported row.kind field, making it non-functional despite having correct help text capitalization.

candidate.rationale: Candidate A implements all 10 components with correct props and functional fidelity to the reference. Its table-responsive accepts headers, form-select handles associative arrays, and breadcrumb uses the expected item structure. The exam layout covers anti-cheat and timer requirements, though it includes inappropriate non-English text and slightly deviates in flash-message placement. Candidate B misses critical functionality: table-responsive omits the headers prop entirely (a core requirement), form-select breaks on standard associative option arrays, and its breadcrumb demo shows broken usage. While B's exam anti-cheat is slightly more robust and its alert wrappers are DRYer, the component bugs and extraneous demo churn make it less correct and complete.

claude.rationale: Candidate A matches the reference structure more closely: standalone alert components, $slot-based exam layout, and correct form-select key-value iteration. Its main flaw is the reversed old() precedence ($value ?? old($name) instead of old($name, $value)), which breaks form resubmission when a default value is passed. Candidate B has correct old() handling but commits more severe errors: form-select iterates options incorrectly (breaking standard key-value usage), table-responsive lacks the required headers prop, exam layout uses @yield/@section paradigm instead of $slot, references a non-existent route, and has a duplicate JS variable. Candidate A's single functional bug is outweighed by Candidate B's multiple structural and functional deviations.

candidate.rationale: Candidate A fatally early-returns only the debug panel when import.meta.env.DEV is true, completely suppressing the overlap banner during development and breaking the component's core functionality. Candidate B satisfies all major requirements—panel renders in DEV only, is collapsible, shows pair details, highlights matches, and appears regardless of overlap state—but deviates from the reference by adding a redundant separate query instead of extending the existing one, and incorrectly computes 'mine' and 'theirs' counts as unique city names rather than total itinerary lengths.

claude.rationale: Candidate A correctly shows the debug panel alongside the working overlap banner (both no-overlap and overlap cases), meets all acceptance criteria, and only deviates from the reference in using a separate extra query and computing mine/theirs counts as unique cities rather than raw itinerary counts. Candidate B has a critical bug: an early return inside `if (import.meta.env.DEV)` completely suppresses the overlap banner in development mode, breaking the core feature. It also spawns three separate queries and computes pairs inline on every render.

candidate.rationale: Candidate B is architecturally sound with valid Prisma schema and proper server components, but loses points for nested forms in the detail page (breaking delete) and omitting date handling in create/update actions. Candidate A fatally imports Prisma and auth into a client component (list page), causing a build/runtime failure and potential credential exposure, and introduces a Prisma schema syntax error with an extra closing brace. While A's actions are more complete, the critical client-side Prisma misuse makes it far less correct.

claude.rationale: Candidate B is substantially closer to the reference. Its schema is correct (SiteStatus enum in proper position, all three indexes including @@index([organizationId]), cascade delete), both pages are proper server components, and all four server actions accept FormData matching the reference pattern and use requireRole(). Its gaps: openedAt/closedAt are omitted from both createSite and updateSite raw data/schema, notes uses <Input> instead of <Textarea> on the edit page, the delete form is nested inside the update form (invalid HTML), and the revalidatePath in createSite incorrectly appends organizationId. Candidate A has more severe/blocking problems: the schema diff has a stray closing brace that would break the KbArticle model; the @@index([organizationId]) standalone index is missing; sites/page.tsx is marked 'use client' yet tries to embed async server component functions (SiteListWrapper calls auth() and prisma — illegal in a client module); the server action signatures deviate from FormData-only pattern (updateSite takes siteId as a positional argument, setSiteStatus/deleteSite take siteId directly rather than from FormData) making them incompatible with plain HTML form submissions; and auth() is used directly instead of requireRole().

candidate.rationale: Candidate A correctly preserves per-service cookie grouping for cobalt.json and returns Netscape metadata (domain, secure, expiry) matching the reference intent, despite missing tests, lacking errors='replace', and setting valid_json=True for missing cobalt files. Candidate B violates the explicit per-service requirement by flattening all cobalt cookie names into a single global list, and its Netscape response omits metadata fields, making it less correct and aligned. Both omitted tests and used broad exception handling.

claude.rationale: Candidate A has the correct netscape 'cookies' key returning list-of-dicts matching the reference, and a more complete per-service cobalt structure, but critically uses 'cobalt_json' instead of 'cobalt' as the top-level key (failing all reference tests) and lacks the 'parse' wrapper. Candidate B uses the correct 'cobalt' top-level key but replaces the netscape 'cookies' list-of-dicts with a flat 'cookie_names' list of strings, loses the per-service cobalt breakdown, and also omits the 'parse' wrapper. Neither adds tests. Candidate A's netscape structure aligns better with the reference despite the wrong cobalt key name.

candidate.rationale: Candidate A correctly switches to the Vite dev server, removes the custom backend and unused dependencies, adds the Vercel SPA routing config, updates the logo proxy to the public service, and includes the required useEffect cleanup for the QR container. Candidate B matches the infrastructure and proxy changes but omits the useEffect cleanup entirely. Both candidates miss adding the imageOptions block to qrCode.update and do not remove the legacy downloadQR function as shown in the reference.

claude.rationale: Both candidates correctly update package.json, delete server.ts, add vercel.json, and swap the proxy URL to images.weserv.nl. Candidate B additionally adds the useEffect cleanup (returning a teardown that empties the container), which is explicitly listed in the acceptance criteria and matches the reference patch. Neither candidate implements the imageOptions block (crossOrigin, margin, imageSize, hideBackgroundDots) or removes the downloadQR function, which are also required. The proxy URL encoding approach (encodeURIComponent vs reference's protocol-stripping + &n=-1) slightly deviates in both. Candidate B is the better solution due to the cleanup addition.

candidate.rationale: Candidate A delivers all required components—cc-explore/build/continue wrappers, session-start and end-conversation skills, statusline script, configure.sh, cc-doctor, and .gitignore updates—so it is functionally complete. However, it deviates from the reference in several details: the .gitignore exception path for canonical/CLAUDE.md is incorrect (uses 'canonical/CLAUDE.md' instead of the full repo-relative path), the statusline uses fragile grep/sed rather than jq to parse JSON, it introduces an extra canonical/settings-sandbox.json not present in the reference, and it writes .cc-mode to $HOME in build mode rather than the repo root. Candidate B provides only .gitignore and configure.sh; while those two files are reasonably aligned with the reference, the patch completely omits the shell functions, skills, statusline command, and doctor script, making it severely incomplete.

claude.rationale: Candidate A is extremely incomplete: it only touches .gitignore and configure.sh while missing every core deliverable (cc-functions.sh, statusline-command.sh, both skill files, doctor script, canonical CLAUDE.md, settings.json, etc.). Its .gitignore ordering is also broken—the negation `!software/development/claude-code/canonical/CLAUDE.md` appears before the final `*CLAUDE.md` rule, so the canonical file would still be excluded. Its configure.sh omits `require_command jq`, doesn't create the `~/.claude/shell/` parent dir before symlinking cc-functions.sh, and uses bare `ln -s` which fails if a target already exists. Candidate B creates substantially more of the required files: cc-functions.sh, statusline-command.sh, both SKILL.md bookends, and a doctor script. However, it has multiple notable divergences from the reference: (1) .gitignore uses `!canonical/CLAUDE.md` (a root-relative path that won't protect the deeply-nested file); (2) cc-functions.sh uses `MODE=explore` instead of `mode=exploration` and places worktrees at `~/.claude/worktrees/` instead of a sibling dir, breaking cc-continue compatibility; (3) the sandbox settings written by cc-explore are syntactically different from the reference's `{"sandbox":{"enabled":true,"failIfUnavailable":true}}`; (4) statusline-command.sh reads only one line with `read`, not a full JSON document, and always reads `$HOME/.cc-mode` rather than walking up from the cwd embedded in the JSON; (5) doctor.sh uses `set -euo pipefail` (the reference deliberately uses only `set -uo pipefail` so all checks run even on failure); (6) canonical/settings.json is a minimal 12-line stub vs the reference's 58-line file with full permissions/plugin/statusLine config; (7) missing render-transcript.sh, canonical/CLAUDE.md content, plane-api skill, and canonical README. Despite these issues, Candidate B delivers more functional surface area and is closer to the reference intent.

candidate.rationale: Candidate B more closely matches the reference direction by fully stripping unused plan fields and rendering a uniform, static 'Coming soon' placeholder on every card. Candidate A retains unnecessary conditional CTA styling and dead data properties (emails, campaigns), introducing unrelated churn and deviating from the intended clean, static placeholder.

claude.rationale: Both candidates correctly remove 'use client', useState, useEffect, Supabase client, getCheckoutUrl, and the loading state, and both update the footer text correctly. The key differentiator is the 'Coming soon' CTA styling: Candidate B uses uniform gray placeholder styling (border + gray text) for all plan cards, matching the reference's intent of a neutral static placeholder. Candidate A incorrectly applies dynamic styling to the 'Coming soon' button (black background for the highlighted plan, border for others), treating it like an active CTA rather than a disabled placeholder — inconsistent with the task's goal of a simple static message. Minor shared deviations from the reference (keeping `animate-fade-in` and `grid-cols-1 sm:grid-cols-3` instead of `px-6 py-16` and `grid-cols-3`) are present in both and have minimal impact.

candidate.rationale: Candidate A fulfills all core requirements: updated URLs, expanded challenge/login detection, cookie restoration with fallback, and cookie sync after both search and re-login. claude.rationale:
Both candidates correctly remove the six history-mirror recency checks from retail_signal_coverage_agent.py and create a new history coverage agent. However, each has distinct critical flaws.

Candidate A defects: (1) The DB_DEFINITIONS use `freshness_threshold_hours: 172800`, which is a copy-paste bug — 172800 is the threshold in *seconds* (48 h), not hours; this makes every DB appear perpetually fresh. (2) The systemd service hardcodes `--no-post` in ExecStart, meaning the agent will *never* POST to pi4b in production — a fundamental functional break. (3) The systemd timer uses `OnUnitActiveSec=5min` (relative drift) instead of the required `OnCalendar=*:00,05,10,...:55:30` (absolute wall-clock marks). (4) `time` is not imported at the module level, causing a NameError in `_age_hours`. (5) The extras_fn resolver via `globals().get()` is fragile.

Candidate B defects: (1) It does not produce any systemd .service or .timer files at all, missing two of the six acceptance criteria. (2) The payload omits `fresh_count` and `total_count` fields present in the reference. (3) `post_payload` re-raises on HTTP failure rather than returning a boolean gracefully. (4) It also removes `_run_recency_epoch_check` and the `recency_epoch` dispatch branch from signal_coverage — additional churn beyond the reference, though arguably clean.

Candidate B's agent implementation is substantially more correct: freshness thresholds are in proper hours (e.g., 25 h daily, 2 h hourly), collectors are implemented as plain callables resolved via a COLLECTORS dict (no fragile globals lookup), the service will actually POST, and the heartbeat pattern matches the reference. The extra signal_coverage cleanup (removing the now-dead `_run_recency_epoch_check`) is reasonable but deviates from the reference. The absence of systemd files is a significant gap, but a fixable omission, whereas Candidate A's --no-post in the service is a silent production defect, and the 172800-hours threshold bug is semantically wrong.

It deviates from the reference in some layout details (e.g., hardcoded badge colors, integer hoverScale, duplicate alpha sliders in surfaces) but nothing is broken. Candidate B correctly implements all major requirements—expanded challenge/login-failure detection, parallel solver without deadlock, URL updates, cookie restore on init, and post-search/re-login cookie sync—despite some minor issues: dead-code churn (unused reflect block in searchWithScraper), slightly less efficient solver without early termination, and missing a user save during cookie restore.

claude.rationale: Candidate A covers most requirements (expanded challenge detection, updated search/login URLs, cookie restore with fallback, cookie sync after search/re-login), though its parallel solveBotChallenge has a potential deadlock (workers blocked on full resultChan after receiver exits) and the cookie restore adds an extra validation request not in the reference. Candidate B has critical Go compilation errors: unused 'nonceChan' variable and unused 'scraperValue' (reflect) variable, which would prevent the code from building entirely. It also lacks cookie sync after re-login retry and has dead/broken code in searchWithScraper. Candidate A is clearly superior despite its implementation imperfections.

Both candidates deviate from the reference by inlining components instead of creating separate AudioVisualizer.jsx and TypewriterText.jsx files, and neither implements the `onUpdate` scroll callback the reference uses.

Candidate B is further off architecturally: it imports non-existent pymodbus APIs (ServerCallback, register_callback) and returns a Request object where a Response belongs, making its interception layer invalid. It also strips important operational docstring context and still omits read chunking. Neither is fully correct, but A contains fewer fatal errors and less API misuse.

candidate.rationale: Candidate A satisfies the core PWA requirements more accurately: the banner appears after 3 seconds as specified, uses correct Japanese copy, and shows text-only instructions on iOS without an extraneous button. claude.rationale: Both candidates implement the core features (PWA banner, multi-level URL fallback, localStorage cache), but Candidate A aligns more closely with the reference. Key points: (1) Candidate A uses the correct localStorage suppression key 'honestBabyInstallDismissed' matching the reference; Candidate B uses 'honestBabyPWADismissed', which diverges. (2) Candidate A consolidates iOS detection and suppression checks in a single useEffect (closer to reference), while Candidate B splits into two effects creating a potential race condition where the banner fires after 3s for non-iOS users even when no beforeinstallprompt is available. (3) Candidate B's handlePWAInstall does not set the suppression flag if the user declines, allowing repeated prompts. (4) Both miss the formatDbProduct helper for normalizing Supabase data, and both position the banner at 'bottom-4' instead of the reference's 'bottom-[72px]' (which keeps it above the nav bar). (5) Candidate A's localStorage cache eviction removes only the single oldest key on overflow, which is acceptable. Candidate A is the stronger implementation overall.
